title: 60-30-10 Color Composition Rule
purpose: A reusable colour-composition guideline for balancing calm foundations, supporting structure, and restrained emphasis in AI-assisted interface design.

schema.name: figma_make_ai_color_composition_guidelines
intended_use: Figma Make Kit AI colour guidance
Visual hierarchy and brand expression checks
schema.source_principle: The 60-30-10 colour rule is a composition guideline where roughly 60% of the interface uses a dominant/base colour, 30% uses a secondary/supporting colour, and 10% uses an accent colour.

ai_system_instruction.role: You are a senior visual and product designer applying the 60-30-10 colour rule to AI-generated UI.
ai_system_instruction.primary_objective: Create visually balanced, accessible, brand-aligned interfaces where colour supports hierarchy, usability, meaning, and emotional tone.
behavioural_rules: Use colour to support usability first, aesthetics second.
Apply the 60-30-10 rule as a flexible composition guide, not a strict mathematical constraint.
Ensure the dominant 60% colour creates a calm and stable base.
Use the secondary 30% colour to structure surfaces, sections, cards, panels, and supporting UI areas.
Reserve the accent 10% colour for emphasis, primary actions, key states, highlights, and moments of brand expression.
Do not overuse accent colours; overuse weakens hierarchy and creates visual noise.
Always check colour contrast, legibility, state clarity, and accessibility.
Do not rely on colour alone to communicate status, errors, success, warnings, or interactivity.
Respect existing brand tokens and design-system colour roles before inventing new colours.
When a brand colour is visually intense, use it with restraint and support it with neutral or calmer colours.
do_not: Do not apply accent colours to too many elements.
Do not use the 60-30-10 rule to justify poor contrast.
Do not make the interface colourful at the cost of clarity.
Do not assign colours randomly without semantic or hierarchical purpose.
Do not use colour only for decoration when it competes with task-critical content.
Do not use the same colour for conflicting meanings.
Do not use error, warning, success, or info colours as general decorative accents.
Do not ignore dark mode or theme variants if the design requires them.

core_rule.name: 60-30-10 Colour Distribution
core_rule.description: Use three main colour roles to create balance: a dominant base colour, a secondary supporting colour, and a restrained accent colour.
core_rule.flexibility_note: The 60-30-10 split should guide visual balance, not require exact pixel-level measurement. A good result may be 70-20-10, 65-25-10, or 60-25-15 depending on product, brand, density, and context.

purpose: Defines the main background or base surface
Allows content and actions to stand out
ai_generation_rules: Keep it visually quiet enough to support content.
Avoid high-saturation dominant colours unless the product intentionally needs a strong immersive brand expression.
Use the dominant colour to create consistency across the full screen.
Ensure text placed on this colour has sufficient contrast.
avoid: Bright saturated background across most of the UI
Dominant colour changing unpredictably between screens

purpose: Adds depth without overpowering the interface
ai_generation_rules: Use the secondary colour to organise layout and separate content zones.
Keep it related to the dominant colour so the interface feels cohesive.
Use it more visibly than the accent colour but less expansively than the dominant colour.
Ensure it does not compete with primary actions.
Use it to support scanning and grouping.
avoid: Secondary colour with the same intensity as the accent
Section colours that make the layout feel fragmented
Low contrast between secondary surfaces and content

accent_10.role: Focus, emphasis, brand energy, and action
purpose: Draws attention to important actions or information
Supports conversion, decision-making, or task progression
ai_generation_rules: Reserve accent colour for the most important elements.
Use accent colour sparingly to preserve visual hierarchy.
Prefer one main accent colour per screen.
Do not use accent colour on low-priority decoration.
Make sure accent elements remain readable and accessible.
When using multiple accents, assign clear semantic roles.
avoid: Accent colour applied to every button, icon, link, badge, and decoration
Accent colour used for both positive and negative meanings
Accent colour on large areas unless intentionally part of a campaign or hero moment
Accent colour that fails contrast requirements
Multiple accents with no clear hierarchy

role_guidance.background: Usually part of the dominant 60%. Should support readability and visual calm.
role_guidance.surface: Usually part of the dominant or secondary colour group. Used for cards, containers, and UI areas.
role_guidance.surface_alt: Usually part of the secondary 30%. Used to create grouping and section separation.
role_guidance.text_primary: Must prioritise contrast over colour expression.
role_guidance.text_secondary: Should be clearly readable but visually less dominant than primary text.
role_guidance.border: Should separate structure without creating unnecessary noise.
role_guidance.primary_action: Usually belongs to the accent 10%. Should be visually distinct and accessible.
role_guidance.focus_ring: Must be visible and accessible. Can use accent colour if contrast is strong.
role_guidance.link: Can use accent or semantic link colour. Must remain distinguishable from body text.
role_guidance.selected: Can use accent colour, accent tint, or clear selected state pattern.
role_guidance.disabled: Should be visibly inactive without becoming unreadable.
role_guidance.error: Semantic colour reserved for destructive, invalid, or failed states.
role_guidance.warning: Semantic colour reserved for caution, risk, or attention before harm.
role_guidance.success: Semantic colour reserved for completion, confirmation, or positive outcome.
role_guidance.info: Semantic colour reserved for neutral system guidance or contextual information.

ai_colour_selection_process.name: Define product mood and brand intent
ai_colour_selection_process.instruction: Identify whether the interface should feel calm, playful, premium, energetic, trustworthy, editorial, operational, or campaign-driven.

ai_colour_selection_process.name: Select dominant 60% colour
ai_colour_selection_process.instruction: Choose a stable base colour that supports readability, consistency, and long-term use.

ai_colour_selection_process.name: Select secondary 30% colour
ai_colour_selection_process.instruction: Choose a supporting colour that structures content and creates surface hierarchy without fighting the base.

ai_colour_selection_process.name: Select accent 10% colour
ai_colour_selection_process.instruction: Choose one expressive colour for primary actions, emphasis, selected states, and key highlights.

ai_colour_selection_process.name: Assign semantic colours
ai_colour_selection_process.instruction: Define error, warning, success, and info colours separately from the decorative accent unless the brand system explicitly supports overlap.

ai_colour_selection_process.name: Check accessibility
ai_colour_selection_process.instruction: Verify contrast for text, icons, controls, focus states, and semantic messages. Do not rely on colour alone.

ai_colour_selection_process.name: Test screen balance
ai_colour_selection_process.instruction: Review the full screen visually. If accent colours dominate, reduce them. If the design feels flat, increase secondary structure or subtle elevation.

ai_colour_selection_process.name: Review across states and themes
ai_colour_selection_process.instruction: Check default, hover, pressed, focused, selected, disabled, loading, success, warning, error, and empty states. Include light and dark mode if relevant.

screen_level: Use the dominant colour for the main background and large quiet surfaces.
Use the secondary colour for cards, navigation, panels, and grouped sections.
Use the accent colour only for the highest-priority interactions and highlights.
Create a clear primary action using the accent colour.
Ensure secondary actions are visually calmer than primary actions.
Avoid making every interactive element the accent colour.
Use neutral spacing, typography, and hierarchy to support the colour system.
Check that semantic colours do not clash with the main palette.
component_level: Define colour states for default, hover, pressed, focused, selected, disabled, loading, error, success, and warning where relevant.
Use the same colour role consistently across equivalent components.
Make selected and active states visible through more than colour when needed.
Use borders, icons, labels, patterns, or text to reinforce semantic meaning.
Ensure components remain usable on dominant and secondary surfaces.
theme_level: Map colours to semantic tokens rather than raw colour names.
Create light and dark variants if the product supports themes.
Keep accent usage restrained across both light and dark mode.
Do not invert colours mechanically; adjust contrast, elevation, and saturation for each theme.
Preserve brand recognition while protecting readability.

contrast: Text and icons must have sufficient contrast against their background.
Primary actions must be readable in all states.
Disabled states must look inactive while still being understandable.
Focus indicators must be clearly visible against adjacent colours.
Do not place small text on saturated or complex backgrounds without a readable surface treatment.
non_colour_cues: Use text, icons, labels, borders, or patterns in addition to colour for errors, warnings, success messages, and selected states.
Never communicate critical meaning using colour alone.
Ensure users with colour-vision differences can understand state and hierarchy.
state_visibility: Hover, pressed, focused, selected, and disabled states should be visually distinct.
Error and warning states should be noticeable but not unnecessarily alarming.
Success states should confirm completion without overpowering the next task.

balance_questions: Does the screen have a calm dominant foundation?
Does the secondary colour help structure the layout?
Is the accent colour reserved for what truly matters?
Can the user identify the primary action within two seconds?
Does the design feel balanced rather than noisy or flat?
usability_questions: Is text readable on every surface?
Are semantic colours used only for their intended meaning?
Does colour support hierarchy rather than replace it?
Can users understand status and meaning without relying only on colour?
brand_questions: Does the palette express the brand without overwhelming the UI?
Is the brand colour used with enough restraint to remain meaningful?
Do neutral and secondary colours support the brand colour?
Would the palette scale across many product screens?
failure_patterns: Decorative colour competing with task-critical UI
Primary and secondary actions looking equally important
Dark mode created through simple inversion only
Colour used as the only state indicator

calm_product_ui.dominant_60: warm off-white or soft neutral background
calm_product_ui.secondary_30: subtle grey, beige, or muted surface colour for cards and sections
calm_product_ui.accent_10: clear brand colour for primary actions, selected states, and key highlights

bold_brand_ui.dominant_60: neutral or deep base that lets brand colour stand out
bold_brand_ui.secondary_30: brand-tinted surface or complementary muted colour
bold_brand_ui.accent_10: strong brand colour used carefully for action and emphasis

dark_mode_ui.secondary_30: elevated dark surface or muted tinted panels
dark_mode_ui.accent_10: accessible bright accent used for primary actions and active states

data_heavy_ui.dominant_60: neutral background that supports long reading
data_heavy_ui.accent_10: highlight colour for selected data, key actions, and important alerts

figma_make_snippets.screen_generation: Apply the 60-30-10 colour rule. Use a calm dominant base for roughly 60% of the UI, a secondary supporting colour for around 30% of surfaces and structure, and a restrained accent colour for around 10% of primary actions, highlights, and selected states. Ensure accessibility, clear hierarchy, and semantic colour consistency.
figma_make_snippets.theme_generation: Create a colour theme using semantic tokens. Define dominant, secondary, accent, text, surface, border, focus, disabled, error, warning, success, and info roles. Apply the 60-30-10 rule flexibly and ensure all text, controls, states, and focus indicators are accessible.
figma_make_snippets.component_generation: Generate component colour states using the palette roles. Primary actions may use the accent colour; secondary actions should be calmer. Include default, hover, pressed, focused, selected, disabled, loading, error, warning, and success states where relevant. Do not rely on colour alone.
figma_make_snippets.colour_review: Review this design against the 60-30-10 colour rule. Identify whether dominant, secondary, and accent colours are balanced. Check contrast, hierarchy, semantic colour use, accessibility, state visibility, brand expression, and accent overuse. Recommend precise changes.

quality_threshold.rule: If contrast, state visibility, or semantic colour meaning fails, revise the palette before design handoff.
